title: Calendula compress for minor cuts and grazes
subtitle: A vulnerary infusion for first-aid wound care
excerpt: A strong infusion of dried calendula flowers, cooled and applied as a compress to minor cuts, grazes, and slow-healing skin abrasions. Calendula's vulnerary and antimicrobial properties are among the most documented in the western herbal tradition.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), marigold entry, vulnerary action on wounds, antiseptic properties of the flowers. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), calendula for wounds and inflammation. EMA HMPC monograph on Calendula officinalis flos (2017), confirms traditional use for minor wounds, small cuts, abrasions, and mild inflammatory skin conditions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make fresh for each session. The compress liquid does not store safely, make only as much as you will use in one application session.

recipeTools.notes: Or any heatproof jug for the initial infusion.
For the compress itself, clean cloth, large enough to fold several times.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally used to promote wound healing and tissue repair. From the Latin vulnerarius, related to wounds.

content.text: A strong calendula infusion, steeped and cooled, used to soak a muslin cloth that is then held against a minor cut or graze. Calendula is the herbal tradition's foremost

herb, Maud Grieve (1931) gives it for wound healing and antiseptic action; the EMA's 2017 monograph confirms traditional use for minor wounds, cuts, abrasions, and mild inflammatory skin conditions. The compress is the simplest external preparation: an infusion made hot, cooled to a comfortable temperature, and applied to the skin as a soaked cloth held in place for five to ten minutes. The liquid version is gentler and more soothing on fresh wounds than the calendula salve, it cools, cleans, and delivers the herb's wound-healing constituents directly.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Asteraceae allergy: calendula is in the Asteraceae (daisy) family. Readers with a known allergy to ragweed, chrysanthemum, chamomile, or related plants should patch-test before use, apply a small amount of the cooled infusion to the inner forearm and wait 24 hours.

Wound depth: this preparation is for minor surface cuts and grazes only. Deep wounds, puncture wounds, wounds that will not stop bleeding, animal bites, and wounds with signs of infection (increasing redness, spreading, warmth, pus) all need medical assessment.

Do not apply to the eye area. For eye compresses, use the chamomile eye compress preparation instead.

Keep the compress liquid clean. Do not double-dip the compress cloth, use one cloth per application.

items.prepNote: just off the boil, the infusion will be cooled before use

content.text: Place {{dried-calendula-flowers}} of dried calendula flowers in the teapot. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over, put the lid on, and steep for 15 minutes. This is a stronger steep than a drinking infusion, the extra time extracts more of the wound-healing and antimicrobial constituents. Strain through a sieve into a clean jug.

Allow the infusion to cool to a comfortable, just-warm temperature, not hot. Fold a clean piece of muslin cloth several times to create a pad. Submerge the cloth in the infusion and squeeze out the excess. Apply the soaked compress gently to the minor cut or graze and hold in place for five to ten minutes. Repeat with a freshly soaked cloth two to three times in the same session.

Apply the compress two to three times daily until the wound has closed and there is no sign of infection. Make a fresh infusion for each session, do not reuse yesterday's liquid. For wounds that have not begun to heal within three days, or that show any sign of infection, see your GP or nurse.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not use on deep wounds, puncture wounds, wounds from animal bites, wounds that won't stop bleeding, or any wound that has signs of infection. Skip if you have an Asteraceae allergy. See your doctor for wounds in high-risk locations (face, hands, over joints), wounds in people with diabetes or circulatory problems, or any wound in a child that seems disproportionately painful or slow to heal.
